cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The user is asking about what the history experts' statement proves regarding the "Happy Birthday" song. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to look back at the passage. The key part is where it says that history experts say there's no record of who wrote the "Happy Birthday to You" lyrics and that the public was singing it long before it was printed and owned by a company. The main point here is that the song was being used by the public before any company claimed it. The article mentions Warner Music Group has claimed the copyright since 1988, but the history experts' statements suggest the song was in the public domain long before that. So, the lawyers are arguing that because of this history, the song belongs to everyone, which would mean Warner has no right to charge fees. The question is about what the experts' statements prove. Option A says it doesn't belong to Warner Music Group. That seems to align with the experts' statements that the public was using it before the company owned it. Option B claims it has more than 200 years' history. The song was written in 1893, so that's over 100 years, but not 200. The passage doesn't mention 200 years, so B is probably incorrect. Option C says it was initially owned by another company. The passage doesn't mention another company owning it before Warner. The sisters wrote "Good Morning to All," but the lyrics changed, and there's no record of who wrote the birthday version. So C doesn't fit. Option D states it has always been very popular.
